claims.claim: The finite tie-aware Best-of-N law exactly predicts selected utility from an empirical score/utility pool; Monte Carlo agrees within sampling error.
In the controlled hidden-mode toy, high N can inflate selected imagined latent value while selected executed real utility stagnates or drops.

A CPU-trained small RSSM-style model with encoder, recurrent state, stochastic prior/posterior, decoder, reward head, and value head reproduces the latent-real selection mismatch in a smoke-scale learned setting.
Under ambiguous hidden modes, high-N latent selection concentrates in optimistic imagined modes even when many selected candidates execute in blocked/slip/heavy modes.
Varying N and H shows where longer or fatter imagination worsens latent-real mismatch.
RSSM-specific repairs can substantially recover selected-tail real utility over raw latent value selection; near-oracle is reported only when achieved.
No external benchmark, full Dreamer benchmark, or real-world robotic benchmark is implemented in this repo.
claims.evidence: Repository scope and scripts are toy/RSSM-style CPU experiments only.

claims.claim: Real robot validation is unsupported and must not be claimed.
claims.evidence: No robot datasets, robot execution adapters, or robot result artifacts exist.

claims.claim: Forbidden universal or real-robot claims are blocked.

blocked_phrases: This is just a renamed WAM project.

forbidden_claims: This is just a renamed WAM project.
